column2: — Lilichka stretched out her lips toward the bald cat cheeks.
The cat looked at them with the gaze not of a kitten, but of a half-monster, and made some sounds — something between a meow and a purr.
— God, how hideous it is, — said Miyechka, afraid to touch the cat's skin.
— It was sort of defective there.
— Well, I can see that.
— Yeah, — Lilichka went on, — look, its hind legs are furry for some reason.
— God.
— Oh, what's the matter with you?
It's a good thing.
It'll be warmer for him.
— Is it a he or a she?
— asked Miyechka.
— And that's another plus!
Look!
— Lilichka lifted the kitten in front of Miyechka's face.
She recoiled.
— I see.
It's a she.
— What shall we name her?
— I don't know, Lilya.
It's very hideous.
— Oh, it's fine!
You'll get used to it!
— And grandmother?
— She'll get used to it too!
— So you were busy, as usual, saving the wretched?
— asked Miyechka, daring to stroke the kitten with a finger.
— Well, they couldn't sell it.
Its legs, you see, are defective.
— Its legs are actually the normal part.
Lilichka set the kitten on the floor.
It still walked unsteadily and crawled across the carpet like a bald little ball.
— Lilya, — Miyechka spoke up, — this is the most hideous thing I've ever seen in my life.
— Let's name her Jacqueline.
— Jacqueline?
— Yeah, like Kennedy's wife.
Grandmother liked her.
— Let grandmother name her, — said Miyechka.
The grandmother named the kitty Yulya.
But first she asked:
— God, is it sick?
Miyechka had to tell the grandmother that yes, it was sick and its fur would grow in.
— Lilya, — said Miyechka, — we have to prepare grandmother, we can't say that it'll stay like this.
Let her think the fur will grow back.
The grandmother especially ordered the pregnant Marta not to pick up the kitten, in case it was contagious.
Lilichka argued that it wasn't contagious.
Marta hadn't expressed any desire to go near Yulya anyway.
She was furious after the examination at the doctor's, cursed the doctor, Maria, who had taken her there, cursed everyone.
— I have no use for these children at all!
— she shouted.
— I could have gotten rid of them long ago, and that's what I should have done!
— Marta, — the grandmother said calmly, — don't say such things…
— And I don't care, — she shouted.
— I don't want them, I don't need them!
No one argued with Marta.
Maria simply left at such moments.
The girls moved closer to each other, and the grandmother sat in silence.
And only Lusya's crying stopped Marta for a while.

The little girl burst into tears when she saw the kitty, and only after a long talk with Lilichka, after she'd shown how sweetly Yulya fiddled with a thread with her two furry and two bald little paws, did the child calm down.
And once she even set Yulya on her thin little knees.
The grandmother, of course, grieved for the ginger cat.
Though she did admit that it had been time for him to die.
— Just as it's time for me, — she added, as expected.
She found it distasteful to pick Yulya up and, treating her as sick, kept her in a box at the foot of her bed.
But the kitten kept clambering out and crawling toward her.
— You know, girls, when you're ninety-six, it's a bit strange to get yourself animals, because they have every chance of outliving you, — said the grandmother.
— But this poor thing doesn't look like it'll make it to autumn.
The poor thing, however, ate, ran around, jumped and was beginning to cast doubt on its illness.
Marta, despite the grandmother's warning, began to pick Yulya up, and Lusya no longer cried when she saw the kitty yawn and its whole body wrinkle up.
The grandmother pitied the animal the way only a woman who has raised children, grandchildren and great-grandchildren can pity a living creature.
More and more often she took the kitty in her arms.
And once she was found sleeping during the day with the kitty at her side — she had swaddled the kitty in her white downy shawl, from which only its bald cheeks peeked out.
And the ginger one vanished the way only cats know how.

For these free cats, who hunt bats at night and on long misty mornings sit imperturbably watching a molehill, ready to hook a mole with a claw and wait for it to suffocate, death often does not exist.
— How can that be?
— said Miyechka.
— Why didn't he come?
The grandmother sat wrapped in her robe.
On her feet — downy socks.
Outside — twenty-two degrees.
Yulya was curled up under her chest, and the grandmother kept her hands on her over a handkerchief.
— It happens.
What happened to the ginger one is what happened to Uncle Yakiv, — said the grandmother.
— What happened to Uncle Yakiv?
— asked Lilichka.
The grandmother was lucky to have Lilichka, who listened to her stories, snatching from them only the images and never remembering the plot.
— Uncle Yakiv died.
— Maybe the ginger one will still come?
— asked Miyechka.
— That's just what we all thought too, that Uncle Yakiv would come, and he didn't.
— If he comes, — said Lilichka, — we'll name him Yakiv.
And so for the first time in his life the ginger one got a chance at a real name, but he never used it.
And he became Yakiv posthumously.
The rainy days went on.
Lusya was carried over the grass wrapped in a blanket.
The little girl was ill.
She didn't want to eat.
She lay there breathing hot.
They tried not to let Marta near her.
— What if it's chickenpox?
— said the grandmother.
— Did I ever have chickenpox?
— asked Marta.
Her boys were stirring.
— We'll have to ask your mother.
— I don't want to talk to her.
— You've been here a month and haven't spoken to her once.
— You manage that splendidly yourself in the raspberry, — replied Marta.
The grandmother fell silent and took up Yulya, who was meowing soundlessly at her feet.
It was the end of July, Yulya was shivering.
— Maybe Lusya caught the sickness from the kitty?
— asked the grandmother.
— Granny...
— Miyechka spoke up.
She sat at the table picking the nuts out of the halva.
— The kitty isn't sick, that's just how she is.
— She'll stay bald, then?
— She'll stay that way.
— And why does she have fur on her hind legs?
— She's defective.
— So she's defective in the places where she's covered with fur?
— Mm-hm.
And so the grandmother learned that Yulya would never recover.
The kitty was allowed to sleep with the feverish Lusya — they lay together, and the shade of their skin was almost equally pale.
Lusya, like a marble doll, slept all the time.
And Marta, Lilichka and Miyechka took turns coming up to her and touching their lips to her forehead, even though Maria tearfully begged Marta not to kiss the sick child.
— I don't care, — Marta would retort.
Mrs. Darochka would come to their house and tell them who had died.
For decades now this had been the main news.
Births were little spoken of, while death was the thing that at least somehow stood out from the everyday.
The news was usually brought by those who were already awaiting death themselves.
And in such places people begin to await it after sixty.
After the magic figure 'six' at the start, no one feels sorry anymore, no one says that someone went young.
That one's already lived his fill.
After death all the alcoholics, the disabled, the thieves, the scoundrels and the witches suddenly become good people.
Probably this was exactly what was meant in the Bible about love, forgiveness and eternal life.
— He was such a good man, because such rain fell when they buried him, — Mrs. Darochka would say about the latest deceased.
— The dear sky wept so for him…
— The dear sun shone so, — she would say another time.
— The dear sun shines only for good people.
The grandmother would nod and was only afraid that one of the girls would bring up the dear sky that wept and drive Mrs. Darochka into a dead-end logical corner.
— And you, look at that, you've grown up, — the grandmother would say to the girls afterward.
— Not a word did anyone say about the dear sky and the dear sun.
Lusya was getting better.
She began to climb out from under the blanket, and with her, more and more often, Yulya came out into the world.
No one remembered there ever being so much rain at this time of year, but it was endless, and everyone milled about in the house.
— In rains like this, — said the grandmother, — it's very easy for everyone to fall out.
— There's not even the strength for that, — said Marta.
And the grandmother restrained herself from answering that she had splendidly found the strength these last days to bark at everyone.
— And imagine, — said Lilichka, — if we were told that we'd live the rest of our lives like this.
— In my case that's very possible, — said the grandmother.
She sat in a soft brown robe.
The kitty lay on her lap.
— Granny, — asked Miyechka, — and why didn't Yakiv come?
— Do you mean the cat or Uncle Yakiv?
— asked Lilichka.
— About the uncle.
— They said he died.
— What do you mean, they said?
— Lilichka asked indignantly.
She listened to every story as if for the first time.
Yakiv was the grandmother's uncle.
He had a handsome mustache and a naval uniform.
— Someone said they'd seen him after he was gone.
There was a war, girls, and he was a sailor.
They said he fought.
He was covered in medals, they said.
— And then?
— And then he came to his wife, she's standing in the doorway with the children, and he says: 'Olena, I'll probably die, because they'll eat me up.'
Olena said he gave her his medals, only she was afraid to show them to anyone, and instead showed everyone the death notice.
She said it was for Uncle Yakiv.
But the death notice wasn't for him.
It was for their son.
And so Uncle Yakiv died, and then he was supposedly in New Zealand.
There, Lilichka, you'll go to Australia and look for Uncle Yakiv.
— I'll look…
— Lilichka said gloomily.
— And Olena waited for him, — said the grandmother.
— She kept his trousers.
And then the youngest grew up and went off to apply to the institute over there.
— In his father's trousers?
— In his father's.
But he didn't tell anyone they were his father's.
First, it was embarrassing, and second — the father was, they said, in New Zealand.
And the youngest, you know, didn't look like the uncle at all.
His mother said that maybe he wasn't even his.
— What do you mean, not his?
— Just so.
There was a war.
— What an argument, — Marta cut in.
